questions.question: What is a magnetic field?
options: The region around a magnet where electric current flows
The region where magnetic force can be detected
The region in an area where many magnets are concentrated
The space where magnetic materials lose their magnetism
questions.explanation: A magnetic field is the space surrounding a magnet within which its magnetic influence (force of attraction or repulsion) can be experienced by another magnet or magnetic material.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a property of magnetic field lines?
options: They emerge from the North pole and enter the South pole outside the magnet
They travel from South to North inside the magnet
They intersect each other at the poles
questions.explanation: Magnetic field lines never intersect. If they did, it would mean two different directions of the magnetic field at the same point, which is impossible. All other statements are true properties of magnetic field lines.

questions.question: Outside a bar magnet, the direction of magnetic field lines is from:
questions.explanation: By convention, magnetic field lines travel from the North pole to the South pole outside the magnet, and from South to North inside the magnet, forming closed loops.

questions.question: The strength of a magnetic field at a point is indicated by:
options: The length of the field lines
The colour of the field lines
The thickness of each field line
questions.explanation: Where field lines are closer together and denser, the magnetic field is stronger, such as near the poles. Where they are farther apart and less dense, the field is weaker, such as far from the magnet. Both closeness and density are valid indicators of field strength.

questions.question: Which instrument is commonly used to plot magnetic field lines?
questions.explanation: A small plotting compass is used to trace magnetic field lines. The compass needle aligns itself with the direction of the magnetic field at each point, allowing the field pattern to be mapped.

questions.question: Which of the following are associated with the cause of the Earth's magnetic field? SELECT ALL THAT APPLY
options: A giant bar magnet at Earth's centre
questions.explanation: The Earth's magnetic field is generated by electric currents produced by the movement of molten iron and nickel in the outer core — this is known as the geodynamo theory. A giant bar magnet is a common misconception. The Moon's gravity causes tides, and solar wind particles interact with the field but do not create it.

questions.question: The Earth's magnetic South pole is located near the geographic:
questions.explanation: The Earth's magnetic South pole is located near the geographic North Pole. This is why the North-seeking pole of a compass needle is attracted toward the geographic North — it is drawn to the Earth's magnetic South pole located there.

questions.question: Magnetic field lines inside a bar magnet travel from:
options: They do not exist inside the magnet
questions.explanation: Inside the magnet, field lines travel from the South pole to the North pole, completing the closed loop. Outside the magnet, they go from North to South.

questions.question: If you sprinkle iron filings around a bar magnet, where will they cluster most densely?
options: At the centre of the magnet
At the poles of the magnet
At the midpoint between centre and poles
questions.explanation: Iron filings concentrate near the poles because the magnetic field is strongest there. This simple experiment is used to visualise the field pattern of a magnet.

questions.question: Two magnetic field lines from the same magnet:
options: Can intersect at the poles only
questions.explanation: Magnetic field lines never intersect. If they did, a compass placed at the intersection point would have to point in two different directions at once, which is physically impossible.

questions.question: The region where the magnetic fields of two magnets cancel each other out, resulting in zero net field, is called a:
questions.explanation: A neutral point (or null point) is a location in a magnetic field where the resultant magnetic field strength is zero, usually occurring where opposing fields from two magnets cancel each other.

questions.question: If you cut a bar magnet in half, the magnetic field lines of each half will:
options: Only emerge from the original North pole piece
questions.explanation: Each half becomes a complete magnet with its own North and South poles. Consequently, each piece will have its own set of closed magnetic field lines, independent of the other half.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You have mastered magnetic fields and field line properties!
scoreMessages.70: Good work! Review the direction of field lines inside vs outside a magnet and Earth's magnetic poles.
scoreMessages.50: Keep practicing! Focus on the properties of field lines, neutral points, and how to visualise fields.
scoreMessages.0: Review the basics: definition of a magnetic field, direction of field lines, Earth's magnetic poles, and field line properties.
